caveats: a: Published BLS occupation counts do not measure time by task or current automation within NAICS 622110.
a: The LMM independent-hospital occupation mix may differ from the national industry mix because small hospitals outsource some specialist and administrative functions.
a: Clinical documentation may be AI-assistable without reducing licensed staffing ratios or bedside coverage.
rho: Predictive-AI adoption is not the same as labor substitution or full workflow implementation.
rho: The HealthIT.gov population is nonfederal acute-care hospitals of all sizes, not the frozen LMM firm lens.
rho: Already-deployed predictive models do not belong in the remaining opportunity measured by a.
e: Hospital ownership, establishment, certification, and firm boundaries do not align cleanly.
e: Nonprofit control can transfer through membership substitution or affiliation without a conventional equity sale.
e: The supplied n value is margin-bridged from broad public data and may include system campuses or entities whose economics cannot transfer independently.
s5: Announced hospital-system transactions have no denominator aligned to independent LMM 622110 firms.
s5: A transaction may cover many hospitals or only a parent entity and may not represent a separately qualifying business transfer.
s5: Distress can cause closure or public rescue rather than an investable control transfer.
q: Medicare IPPS does not describe the full commercial, Medicaid, self-pay, and supplemental-payment mix.
q: Payment form does not directly measure the discounted share of AI benefits retained.
q: Some benefits increase capacity, quality, or clinician retention rather than reducing reported expense.
d5: CMS national expenditure projections are nominal and cover hospital care broadly rather than NAICS 622110 LMM operators.
d5: Constant-quality inpatient quantity is difficult to separate from rising acuity, technology, and prices.
d5: Demand varies sharply between growing urban markets and rural or over-bedded markets.
o: The estimate concerns operator requirement after demand changes, not the amount of labor automation inside the hospital.
o: Hospital-at-home preserves accountable clinical operation but may move revenue and assets outside a traditional hospital facility.
o: Different service lines have materially different site-of-care substitution potential.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring patient episodes reimbursed by Medicare, Medicaid, commercial insurers, employers, and patients through case-based prospective payments, negotiated rates, fee schedules, bundled arrangements, and limited cost-based or supplemental payments.
lens.excluded_activities: Government-owned hospitals, captive or non-control hospital campuses within systems, shell or real-estate-only entities, physician offices, outpatient centers, psychiatric and substance-abuse hospitals, other specialty hospitals, and operators outside the lower-middle-market EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market hospital operating firms repeatedly providing inpatient diagnostic, medical, surgical, nursing, accommodation, and related outpatient services to external patients and payers through a transferable operating business.

a.bridge: BLS May 2024 occupation counts show that registered nurses are by far the largest occupation in general medical and surgical hospitals, followed by nursing assistants, while medical secretaries, managers, laboratory staff, imaging staff, physicians, therapists, pharmacists, and other roles form a mixed workforce. BLS nursing duties separate record review, documentation, care planning, coordination, and test analysis from medicine administration, equipment operation, direct care, and physical patient support. The range maps the former tasks and analogous administrative workflows to not-yet-automated AI exposure while treating bedside intervention, surgery, examinations, transport, cleaning, and accountable clinical judgment as less substitutable. It may overstate exposure where digital tools are already deployed and understate it where documentation and revenue-cycle work remain manual.
caveats: Published BLS occupation counts do not measure time by task or current automation within NAICS 622110.
The LMM independent-hospital occupation mix may differ from the national industry mix because small hospitals outsource some specialist and administrative functions.
Clinical documentation may be AI-assistable without reducing licensed staffing ratios or bedside coverage.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit wage-weighted task diary across independent LMM hospitals, matched to EHR audit logs and current automation inventories for nursing, physicians, revenue cycle, laboratory, imaging, pharmacy, scheduling, and management, would materially change the estimate.
a.rationale: Hospitals contain large pools of structured clinical, scheduling, coding, utilization, supply, and billing work that AI can assist, but wage weight is concentrated in licensed clinicians whose work combines information processing with physical care, communication, escalation, and legal accountability. The base therefore assigns a meaningful minority of current wage-weighted tasks to remaining AI substitution or avoided hiring.

d5.bridge: CMS projects hospital-care spending to grow 5.7% annually from 2025 through 2034 in nominal national expenditure data. That broad all-hospital spending forecast is converted to a five-year constant-price service-demand ratio by discounting expected price and intensity growth and allowing for population aging, utilization, coverage, site-of-care migration, prevention, and local capacity constraints. Nominal spending likely overstates real quantity growth, while the broad hospital category may understate demand in aging or underserved local markets and overstate it where inpatient care shifts to ambulatory or home settings.
caveats: CMS national expenditure projections are nominal and cover hospital care broadly rather than NAICS 622110 LMM operators.
Constant-quality inpatient quantity is difficult to separate from rising acuity, technology, and prices.
Demand varies sharply between growing urban markets and rural or over-bedded markets.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast of admissions and case-mix-adjusted inpatient days for independent general hospitals, split by geography and payer and reconciled to outpatient and hospital-at-home migration, would materially change the ratio.
d5.rationale: An aging population, chronic disease, emergency demand, and the clinical complexity of admitted patients support stable to moderate real demand. Growth is restrained by outpatient surgery, hospital-at-home, shorter stays, prevention, payer utilization management, workforce and bed constraints, rural depopulation, and the fact that projected spending includes prices and service intensity rather than only constant-quality quantities.

caveats: Hospital ownership, establishment, certification, and firm boundaries do not align cleanly.
Nonprofit control can transfer through membership substitution or affiliation without a conventional equity sale.
The supplied n value is margin-bridged from broad public data and may include system campuses or entities whose economics cannot transfer independently.
e.change_evidence: A record-level linkage of the frozen firm universe to CMS certification numbers, AHA ownership and system affiliation, audited financial statements, tax filings, state licenses, real-estate control, and normalized EBITDA would replace this estimate.
e.rationale: The code's operating model normally produces recurring external patient and payer revenue, but eligibility is reduced sharply for state and local government hospitals, federal hospitals, nonprofit or for-profit campuses without separately transferable control, system-owned facilities, hospitals whose normalized EBITDA falls outside the band, distressed facilities without sustainable operations, and records that are establishments rather than independent firms. AHA reports that most community hospitals are nonprofit or government-owned and that most community hospitals are system-affiliated, making the frozen 1,130-firm margin bridge unusually uncertain as a transferable-firm universe.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 622110 ($423,236,584,804) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 622110 ($1,262,818,896,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1580 (margins.json[62]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Hospitals/Healthcare Facilities EBITDA/Sales 15.80% (closest named bucket for provider economics; public support-services bucket at 3.87% reflects a different business model)); revenue/employee $223,601 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 622110); class <5: 273 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,209,125 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $191,042 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 22 firms, avg revenue/firm $5,364,227 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $847,548 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 7 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,929,429 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $304,850 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 179 firms, avg revenue/firm $12,611,380 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,992,598 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 951 firms, avg revenue/firm $38,340,453 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $6,057,792 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 1,141 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,072,426,011 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $169,443,310 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 1,130

caveats: The estimate concerns operator requirement after demand changes, not the amount of labor automation inside the hospital.
Hospital-at-home preserves accountable clinical operation but may move revenue and assets outside a traditional hospital facility.
Different service lines have materially different site-of-care substitution potential.
o.change_evidence: Payer and provider data measuring five-year substitution of current admissions and inpatient days to outpatient, home, virtual, or self-service pathways, together with evidence on autonomous monitoring and regulatory staffing changes, would materially move the estimate.
o.rationale: The industry definition centers on licensed hospitals with inpatient beds, organized medical staffs, diagnostic and treatment services, and specialized facilities and equipment. Software can substitute for administrative steps and some monitoring, while outpatient centers, home care, telehealth, and hospital-at-home can eliminate some admissions. Most surviving inpatient episodes nevertheless require a licensed operator accountable for around-the-clock clinical staffing, facilities, drugs, devices, infection control, emergency response, and patient safety.

q.bridge: CMS states that acute inpatient hospitals are paid predetermined rates per discharge under IPPS, classified by MS-DRG and based on average resource use rather than the specific cost of each case. This fixed-case structure is used as a proxy for the mixed payer book: verified cost savings can be retained within a payment period, but annual Medicare updates, outlier rules, quality adjustments, commercial contract renewals, Medicaid policy, payer audits, shared-savings arrangements, and competitive negotiations transmit part of the benefit. Medicare may overstate fixed-price retention where commercial or Medicaid contracts are cost-linked and understate it where multi-year commercial rates are fixed.
caveats: Medicare IPPS does not describe the full commercial, Medicaid, self-pay, and supplemental-payment mix.
Payment form does not directly measure the discounted share of AI benefits retained.
Some benefits increase capacity, quality, or clinician retention rather than reducing reported expense.
q.change_evidence: Contract- and claim-level analysis for representative LMM hospitals showing revenue by DRG, per diem, cost-based, fee-for-service, capitation, bundled, and shared-savings terms, renewal cadence, labor-cost pass-throughs, denial behavior, and post-productivity repricing would materially narrow the range.
q.rationale: Case-based and negotiated reimbursement generally does not reveal actual labor hours to the payer, supporting near-term retention of implemented administrative and process gains. Discounted five-year retention is moderated by annual rate setting, aggressive payer negotiation, coding and utilization review, quality and readmission penalties, required reinvestment, and the possibility that staffing relief is absorbed as capacity or service resilience rather than cash margin.

rho.bridge: HealthIT.gov reports that 71% of nonfederal acute-care hospitals used predictive AI integrated with the EHR in 2024, but adoption was 37% among independent hospitals versus 86% among system-affiliated hospitals; the fastest-growing uses included billing and scheduling. Those adoption observations are translated into five-year implementation of the narrower, not-yet-automated task opportunity, discounted for the capital, integration, evaluation, bias, privacy, cybersecurity, clinician-acceptance, malpractice, and change-management burdens facing LMM independent operators. The national acute-care evidence likely overstates near-term coverage for small independent hospitals but may understate diffusion of lower-cost generative documentation and administrative tools.
caveats: Predictive-AI adoption is not the same as labor substitution or full workflow implementation.
The HealthIT.gov population is nonfederal acute-care hospitals of all sizes, not the frozen LMM firm lens.
Already-deployed predictive models do not belong in the remaining opportunity measured by a.
rho.change_evidence: System demonstrations and audited before-and-after labor hours from at least fifteen independent LMM hospitals, including model inventory, workflow penetration, override rates, error events, integration cost, staffing changes, and retained clinical controls, would materially narrow the range.
rho.rationale: A hospital can deploy assistive AI in documentation, coding, scheduling, denial management, capacity planning, imaging worklists, and risk review without automating physical care. Five years is long enough for meaningful diffusion, yet broad operational capture is constrained by fragmented systems, scarce informatics staff, validation duties, false-positive costs, workflow disruption, and the need for clinician review and safe fallback.

s5.bridge: Kaufman Hall reports 46 announced hospital and health-system transactions in 2025 and that 43.5% involved a financially distressed party. With 6,100 hospitals reported by AHA, this establishes continuing but sparse and distress-heavy sector transaction activity rather than a direct five-year probability for eligible LMM firms. The range allows multiple facilities per transaction and elevated transfer pressure among smaller standalone hospitals, while excluding internal restructurings, affiliations without control, closures, and failed deals. The announced-system transaction count may understate facility-level control changes but overstate clean, qualifying acquisitions because many transactions are nonprofit affiliations or distress resolutions.
caveats: Announced hospital-system transactions have no denominator aligned to independent LMM 622110 firms.
A transaction may cover many hospitals or only a parent entity and may not represent a separately qualifying business transfer.
Distress can cause closure or public rescue rather than an investable control transfer.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year census of completed U.S. 622110 control changes linked to CMS certification numbers and the frozen LMM population, classifying nonprofit membership transfers, equity sales, asset sales, closures, bankruptcies, and internal system reorganizations, would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Independent hospitals face succession, scale, payer leverage, capital, labor, and compliance pressures that can motivate control transfers, but hospital transactions are slow, regulated, often nonprofit, and frequently involve distressed assets whose operations or liabilities are not cleanly transferable. A qualifying five-year sale probability therefore remains below broad small-business succession anchors despite visible consolidation.

principal_driver: Fixed-case reimbursement and data-rich hospital workflows create room for AI-assisted documentation, revenue-cycle, scheduling, and capacity gains while inpatient demand remains operator-required.
principal_weakness: Most clinical labor remains physical and accountable, and the apparent LMM firm universe is heavily blurred by nonprofit governance, system ownership, public facilities, and hospital-scale economics.

sources.supports: Defines 622110 as licensed general medical and surgical hospitals providing inpatient diagnostic and medical treatment with beds, organized medical staff, and related outpatient, laboratory, imaging, operating-room, and pharmacy services; describes specialized hospital facilities and equipment.
sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau — 2022 NAICS Manual: General Medical and Surgical Hospitals

sources.supports: Lists the largest occupations in general medical and surgical hospitals, including 1,843,130 registered nurses, 425,740 nursing assistants, 195,930 medical secretaries and administrative assistants, 155,670 medical and health services managers, and major laboratory, imaging, physician, respiratory, and pharmacy roles.
sources.title: BLS — May 2024 OEWS Industry Employment Charts

sources.supports: Describes nursing duties spanning assessment, documentation, observation, care planning, coordination, medication and treatment administration, equipment operation, testing, education, physical work, and round-the-clock hospital coverage.
sources.title: BLS Occupational Outlook Handbook — Registered Nurses

sources.supports: Reports 71% predictive-AI adoption among responding nonfederal acute-care hospitals in 2024, 37% adoption among independent hospitals versus 86% among system-affiliated hospitals, use in inpatient risk, billing, and scheduling, and incomplete evaluation across all models.
sources.title: HealthIT.gov — Hospital Trends in the Use, Evaluation, and Governance of Predictive AI, 2023–2024

sources.supports: Reports 6,100 U.S. hospitals and 5,121 community hospitals, including 2,984 nongovernment nonprofit, 1,224 investor-owned, and 913 state and local government community hospitals, with 3,567 community hospitals in systems.
sources.title: American Hospital Association — Fast Facts on U.S. Hospitals, 2026

sources.supports: Reports 46 announced hospital and health-system transactions in 2025, $18.5 billion of transacted revenue, stronger fourth-quarter activity, and a record 43.5% of transactions involving a financially distressed party.
sources.title: Kaufman Hall — Hospital and Health System M&A Activity Ends 2025 With Momentum

sources.supports: Explains that acute inpatient hospital payment is generally predetermined per discharge under MS-DRGs and based on average resource use rather than the specific cost of each individual case, subject to wage and other adjustments.
sources.title: CMS — Inpatient Prospective Payment System

sources.supports: Projects average annual nominal hospital-care spending growth of 5.7% from 2025 through 2034 and shows hospital care remaining about 31% of national health expenditures.
sources.title: CMS — National Health Expenditure Projections 2025–2034

title: General Medical and Surgical Hospitals
